title: Classify discontinuities
description: Given the graph of a function, identify and analyze its points of discontinuity.

assessment_items.question_content:
This is the graph of function $f$. Dashed lines represent asymptotes.

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 3 curves. The first curve starts at (negative 8, 5), moves downward, and ends at a closed circle at (negative 2, 0). The second curve starts at an open circle at (negative 2, negative 3) and moves upward toward a vertical asymptote at x = 4. The third curve moves downward away from the vertical asymptote at x = 4 and ends at about (8, negative 3).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/92e7ad9cf99cd07664c2d93a57eb6758a4a2280a)

**Select the $x$-values at which $f$ has a jump discontinuity.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A jump discontinuity (or step discontinuity) occurs at $x$-values where the one-sided limits exist but aren't equal.

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 3 curves. The first curve starts at (negative 8, 5), moves downward, and ends at a closed circle at (negative 2, 0). The second curve starts at an open circle at (negative 2, negative 3) and moves upward toward a vertical asymptote at x = 4. The third curve moves downward away from the vertical asymptote at x = 4 and ends at about (8, negative 3).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/92e7ad9cf99cd07664c2d93a57eb6758a4a2280a)

Looking at the graph we can see that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to-2^-}f(x)=0$ while $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to-2^+}f(x)=-3$.

Therefore, $f$ has a jump discontinuity at $x=-2$.
$f$ is continuous at $x=0$ and has a vertical asymptote at $x=4$. So these answers are incorrect.
In conclusion, $x=-2$ is the only value where $f$ has a jump discontinuity.

assessment_items.question_content:
This is the graph of function $g$.

![Function g is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 3 curves. The first curve starts in quadrant 3, moves upward to about (negative 3, 6), moves downward, and ends at an open circle at (0, negative 5). The second curve starts at a closed circle at (0, negative 2), moves upward, and ends at a closed circle at (6, 5). The third curve starts at an open circle at (6, 3), moves upward to about (7, 3.8), moves downward, and ends at about (8, 3).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b8a872b4bd10824952eb8c7b0ab4d0bb5c418d48)

**Select the $x$-values at which $g$ has a jump discontinuity.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A jump discontinuity (or step discontinuity) occurs at $x$-values where the one-sided limits exist but aren't equal.

![Function g is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 3 curves. The first curve starts in quadrant 3, moves upward to about (negative 3, 6), moves downward, and ends at an open circle at (0, negative 5). The second curve starts at a closed circle at (0, negative 2), moves upward, and ends at a closed circle at (6, 5). The third curve starts at an open circle at (6, 3), moves upward to about (7, 3.8), moves downward, and ends at about (8, 3).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b8a872b4bd10824952eb8c7b0ab4d0bb5c418d48)

Looking at the graph we can see that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0^-}g(x)=-5$ while $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0^+}g(x)=-2$.

Therefore, $g$ has a jump discontinuity at $x=0$.

Similarly, we can see that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 6^-}g(x)=5$ while $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 6^+}g(x)=3$.

Therefore, $g$ has a jump discontinuity at $x=6$.
$g$ is continuous at $x=2$. So this answer is incorrect.
In conclusion, $x=0$ and $x=6$ are the only values where $g$ has jump discontinuities.

assessment_items.question_content:
This is the graph of function $h$.

![Function h is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 2 curves and a line segment. The first curve starts in quadrant 2, moves downward through about (negative 7, 0) to about (negative 6, negative 0.5), moves upward through (negative 5, 0) to an open circle at (negative 3, 2), moves downward to (0, 1), moves upward, and ends at an open circle at (1, 2). The line segment starts at an open circle at (1, 3), moves upward, and ends at a closed circle at (2, 6). The second curve starts at an open circle at (2, negative 7), moves downward to about (2.5, negative 7.5), moves upward to about (7.3, 3), moves downward, and ends at about (8, 2.3).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/86260ee37e4c9a9862e7d27c7e99c8c08d79118f)

**Select the $x$-values at which $h$ has a jump discontinuity.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A jump discontinuity (or step discontinuity) occurs at $x$-values where the one-sided limits exist but aren't equal.

![Function h is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 2 curves and a line segment. The first curve starts in quadrant 2, moves downward through about (negative 7, 0) to about (negative 6, negative 0.5), moves upward through (negative 5, 0) to an open circle at (negative 3, 2), moves downward to (0, 1), moves upward, and ends at an open circle at (1, 2). The line segment starts at an open circle at (1, 3), moves upward, and ends at a closed circle at (2, 6). The second curve starts at an open circle at (2, negative 7), moves downward to about (2.5, negative 7.5), moves upward to about (7.3, 3), moves downward, and ends at about (8, 2.3).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/86260ee37e4c9a9862e7d27c7e99c8c08d79118f)

Looking at the graph we can see that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 1^-}h(x)=2$ while $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 1^+}h(x)=3$.

Therefore, $h$ has a jump discontinuity at $x=1$.

Similarly, we can see that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2^-}h(x)=6$ while $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2^+}h(x)=-7$.

Therefore, $h$ has a jump discontinuity at $x=2$.
$h$ has a removable discontinuity at $x=-3$. So this answer is incorrect.
In conclusion, $x=1$ and $x=2$ are the only values where $h$ has jump discontinuities.

assessment_items.question_content:
This is the graph of function $f$.

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 2 curves and a closed circle. The first curve starts in quadrant 3, moves upward through an open circle at (negative 6, negative 3) and ends at an open circle at (negative 1, negative 1). The second curve starts at an open circle at (negative 1, negative 6), moves upward to about (3, negative 1.5), moves downward through an open circle at about (5, negative 2.8), and ends in quadrant 4. The closed circle is plotted at (4, 2).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/64916f0dbd40b68bdcdd1a5de49dc6206744e376)

**Select the $x$-values at which $f$ has a jump discontinuity.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A jump discontinuity (or step discontinuity) occurs at $x$-values where the one-sided limits exist but aren't equal.

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 2 curves and a closed circle. The first curve starts in quadrant 3, moves upward through an open circle at (negative 6, negative 3) and ends at an open circle at (negative 1, negative 1). The second curve starts at an open circle at (negative 1, negative 6), moves upward to about (3, negative 1.5), moves downward through an open circle at about (5, negative 2.8), and ends in quadrant 4. The closed circle is plotted at (4, 2).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/64916f0dbd40b68bdcdd1a5de49dc6206744e376)

Looking at the graph we can see that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1^-}f(x)=-1$ while $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1^+}f(x)=-6$.

Therefore, $f$ has a jump discontinuity at $x=-1$.

$f$ has removable discontinuities at $x=-6$ and at $x=4$.

So these answers are incorrect.
In conclusion, $x=-1$ is the only value where $f$ has a jump discontinuity.

assessment_items.question_content:
This is the graph of function $g$. Dashed lines represent asymptotes.

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 2 curves. The first curve starts in quadrant 3, moves upward through an open circle at about (negative 7, negative 5.3), through (negative 3, 0), and toward a vertical asymptote at x = negative 1. The second curve moves upward away from the vertical asymptote at x = negative 1, through (0, 0) to about (1.3, 2.3), moves downward through (6, 0), moves upward through (7, 0), and ends in quadrant 1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e12722f24a777183f6144047d9791268cac74012)

**Select the $x$-values at which $g$ has a jump discontinuity.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A jump discontinuity (or step discontinuity) occurs at $x$-values where the one-sided limits exist but aren't equal.

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 2 curves. The first curve starts in quadrant 3, moves upward through an open circle at about (negative 7, negative 5.3), through (negative 3, 0), and toward a vertical asymptote at x = negative 1. The second curve moves upward away from the vertical asymptote at x = negative 1, through (0, 0) to about (1.3, 2.3), moves downward through (6, 0), moves upward through (7, 0), and ends in quadrant 1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e12722f24a777183f6144047d9791268cac74012)

Looking at the graph we can see that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1^-}g(x)$ and $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1^+}g(x)$ do not exist because of the vertical asymptote.

So this answer is incorrect.

$g$ has a removable discontinuity at $x=-7$ and is continuous at $x=0$.

So these answers are also incorrect.

In conclusion, none of the given $x$-values corresponds to where $g$ has a jump discontinuity.

The answer is none of the above.

assessment_items.question_content:
This is the graph of function $h$.

![Function h is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of a curve and a closed circle. The curve starts in quadrant 2, moves downward through (negative 4, 0) to (0, negative 6), moves upward through open circles at (3, negative 3) and (6, negative 2), and ends in quadrant 1. The closed circle is plotted at (3, negative 5).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/38f8278de98c542407e4e864efb4af8fe2ef2c92)

**Select the $x$-values at which $h$ has a removable discontinuity.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A removable discontinuity occurs at points where the two-sided limit exists but doesn't equal the function value at that point.

![Function h is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of a curve and a closed circle. The curve starts in quadrant 2, moves downward through (negative 4, 0) to (0, negative 6), moves upward through open circles at (3, negative 3) and (6, negative 2), and ends in quadrant 4. The closed circle is plotted at (3, negative 5).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/38f8278de98c542407e4e864efb4af8fe2ef2c92)

Looking at the graph we can see that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3}h(x)=-3$ while $h(3)=-5$.

Therefore, $h$ has a removable discontinuity at $x=3$.

Similarly, we can see that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 6}h(x)=-2$ while $h(6)$ is undefined.

Therefore, $h$ has a removable discontinuity at $x=6$.

$h$ is continuous at $x=0$.

So this answer is incorrect.
In conclusion, $x=3$ and $x=6$ are the only values where $h$ has removable discontinuities.

assessment_items.question_content:
This is the graph of function $f$. Dashed lines represent asymptotes.

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 2 curves and a closed circle. The first curve starts in quadrant 3, moves downward through an open circle at (negative 3, negative 5), and toward a vertical asymptote at x = negative 1. The second curve moves downward away from a vertical asymptote at x = negative 1 to about (0.5, negative 2), and moves upward through (4, 0) and an open circle at (6, 2), and ends in quadrant 1. The closed circle is plotted at (6, 4).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4b384f9e9c9f68035adc0133eaddbf1deb6f8f3e)

**Select the $x$-values at which $f$ has a removable discontinuity.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A removable discontinuity occurs at points where the two-sided limit exists but doesn't equal the function value at that point.

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 2 curves and a closed circle. The first curve starts in quadrant 3, moves downward through an open circle at (negative 3, negative 5), and toward a vertical asymptote at x = negative 1. The second curve moves downward away from a vertical asymptote at x = negative 1 to about (0.5, negative 2), and moves upward through (4, 0) and an open circle at (6, 2), and ends in quadrant 1. The closed circle is plotted at (6, 4).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4b384f9e9c9f68035adc0133eaddbf1deb6f8f3e)

Looking at the graph we can see that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3}f(x)=-5$ while $f(-3)$ is undefined.

Therefore, $f$ has a removable discontinuity at $x=-3$.

Similarly, we can see that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 6}f(x)=2$ while $f(6)=4$.

Therefore, $f$ has a removable discontinuity at $x=6$.

$f$ has a vertical asymptote at $x=-1$.

So this answer is incorrect.
In conclusion, $x=-3$ and $x=6$ are the only values where $f$ has removable discontinuities.

assessment_items.question_content:
This is the graph of function $g$.

![Function g is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 2 curves. The first curve starts at about (negative 8, 0), moves upward to an open circle at (negative 6, 7), moves downward to (negative 4, 0), moves upward, and ends at an open circle at (negative 3, 2). The second curve starts at an open circle at (negative 3, 5), moves downward to about (2, negative 3.3), moves upward to (5, 4), moves downward to (7, 0), moves upward, and ends at about (8, 1).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/598c306da87e2fb27aca67e1410da11f61cc6ced)

**Select the $x$-values at which $g$ has a removable discontinuity.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A removable discontinuity occurs at points where the two-sided limit exists but doesn't equal the function value at that point.

![Function g is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 2 curves. The first curve starts at about (negative 8, 0), moves upward to an open circle at (negative 6, 7), moves downward to (negative 4, 0), moves upward, and ends at an open circle at (negative 3, 2). The second curve starts at an open circle at (negative 3, 5), moves downward to about (2, negative 3.3), moves upward to (5, 4), moves downward to (7, 0), moves upward, and ends at about (8, 1).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/598c306da87e2fb27aca67e1410da11f61cc6ced)

Looking at the graph we can see that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -6}g(x)=7$ while $g(-6)$ is undefined.

Therefore, $g$ has a removable discontinuity at $x=-6$.

$g$ has a jump discontinuity at $x=-3$ and is continuous at $x=5$.

So these answers are incorrect.
In conclusion, $x=-6$ is the only value where $g$ has a removable discontinuity.

assessment_items.question_content:
This is the graph of function $h$.

![Function h is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 3 curves and a closed circle. The first curve starts in quadrant 3, moves downward to about (negative 6.2, negative 6.7), moves upward, and ends at an open circle at (negative 2, 3). The second curve starts at an open circle at (negative 2, negative 4), moves upward, and ends at an open circle at (2, 0). The third curve starts at an open circle at (2, 3), moves downward through an open circle at (4, 2), and ends at about (8, negative 6). The closed circle is plotted at (4, 3).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f65104a9ffc5532b20c8c25a6ea822755a1abf3f)

**Select the $x$-values at which $h$ has a removable discontinuity.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A removable discontinuity occurs at points where the two-sided limit exists but doesn't equal the function value at that point.

![Function h is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 3 curves and a closed circle. The first curve starts in quadrant 3, moves downward to about (negative 6.2, negative 6.7), moves upward, and ends at an open circle at (negative 2, 3). The second curve starts at an open circle at (negative 2, negative 4), moves upward, and ends at an open circle at (2, 0). The third curve starts at an open circle at (2, 3), moves downward through an open circle at (4, 2), and ends at about (8, negative 6). The closed circle is plotted at (4, 3).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f65104a9ffc5532b20c8c25a6ea822755a1abf3f)

Looking at the graph we can see that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 4}h(x)=2$ while $h(4)=3$.

Therefore, $h$ has a removable discontinuity at $x=4$.

$h$ has jump discontinuities at $x=-2$ and at $x=2$.

So these answers are incorrect.
In conclusion, $x=4$ is the only value where $h$ has a removable discontinuity.

assessment_items.question_content:
This is the graph of function $f$. Dashed lines represent asymptotes.

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 1 line segment and 3 curves. The line segment starts at (negative 8, 1), moves horizontally to the right, and ends at an open circle at (negative 5, 1). The first curve starts at a closed circle at (negative 5, negative 4), moves horizontally to the right to (0, negative 4), and then moves upward toward a vertical asymptote at x = 1. The second curve moves downward away from the vertical asymptote at x = 1, through (3, 0), and toward a vertical asymptote at x = 5. The third curve moves upward away from the vertical asymptote at x = 5 to about (6, negative 4), moves nearly horizontally to the right, and ends at (8, negative 4).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9533ea25f5188b6db6832f88706c713d0620c6a2)

**Select the $x$-values at which $f$ has a removable discontinuity.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A removable discontinuity occurs at points where the two-sided limit exists but doesn't equal the function value at that point.

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 1 line segment and 3 curves. The line segment starts at (negative 8, 1), moves horizontally to the right, and ends at an open circle at (negative 5, 1). The first curve starts at a closed circle at (negative 5, negative 4), moves horizontally to the right to (0, negative 4), and then moves upward toward a vertical asymptote at x = 1. The second curve moves downward away from the vertical asymptote at x = 1, through (3, 0), and toward a vertical asymptote at x = 5. The third curve moves upward away from the vertical asymptote at x = 5 to about (6, negative 4), moves nearly horizontally to the right, and ends at (8, negative 4).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9533ea25f5188b6db6832f88706c713d0620c6a2)

Looking at the graph we can see that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -5^{\large{-}}}f(x)=1$ while $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -5^{\large{+}}}f(x)=-4$.

Therefore, $f$ has a jump discontinuity, not a removable discontinuity, at $x=-5$.

$f$ has vertical asymptotes at $x=1$ and at $x=5$.

So these answers are also incorrect.

In conclusion, none of the given $x$-values corresponds to where $f$ has a removable discontinuity.

The answer is none of the above.

assessment_items.question_content:
This is the graph of function $g$. Dashed lines represent asymptotes.

![Function g is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 3 curves. The first curve starts in quadrant 2, moving along the underside of a horizontal asymptote at x = 2, moves upward to about (negative 4, 2.9), and then moves downward toward a vertical asymptote at x = negative 2. The second curve moves downward away from the vertical asymptote at x = negative 2, through about (0, 2) and (1, 0), and then toward a vertical asymptote at x = 3. The third curve moves downward away from the vertical asymptote at x = 3 to about (3.8, 0), moves upward to about (7, 2.2), and then moves downward toward the horizontal asymptote at y = 2.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/bd6aa6433b81fe71474c117fd345796bb3915bee)

**Select the $x$-values at which $g$ has an infinite discontinuity.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: An infinite discontinuity occurs at $x$-values where neither one-sided limit exists because each is unbounded.

![Function g is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 3 curves. The first curve starts in quadrant 2, moving along the underside of a horizontal asymptote at x = 2, moves upward to about (negative 4, 2.9), and then moves downward toward a vertical asymptote at x = negative 2. The second curve moves downward away from the vertical asymptote at x = negative 2, through about (0, 2) and (1, 0), and then toward a vertical asymptote at x = 3. The third curve moves downward away from the vertical asymptote at x = 3 to about (3.8, 0), moves upward to about (7, 2.2), and then moves downward toward the horizontal asymptote at y = 2.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/bd6aa6433b81fe71474c117fd345796bb3915bee)

Looking at the graph we can see that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2^-}g(x)$ approaches negative infinity while $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2^+}g(x)$ approaches positive infinity.

Therefore, $g$ has an infinite discontinuity at $x=-2$.

Similarly, we can see that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3^-}g(x)$ approaches negative infinity while $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3^+}g(x)$ approaches positive infinity.

Therefore, $g$ has an infinite discontinuity at $x=3$.

$g$ is continuous at $x=2$.

So this answer is incorrect.
In conclusion, $x=-2$ and $x=3$ are the only values where $g$ has infinite discontinuities.

assessment_items.question_content:
This is the graph of function $h$. Dashed lines represent asymptotes.

![Function h is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 3 curves and a closed circle. The first curve starts at about (negative 8, negative 1) and moves downward toward a vertical asymptote at x = negative 6. The second curve moves upward away from the vertical asymptote at x = negative 6 to about (negative 4, negative 1) and then moves downward toward a vertical asymptote at x = negative 2. The third curve moves downward away from the vertical asymptote at x = negative 2, through about (1, 0), moves upward through about (2, 0) and an open circle at (4, 4), moves downward through about (7, negative 4), and ends in quadrant 4. The closed circle is plotted at (4, negative 2).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/95714a632ccc4db535abc785a374c85462a0a031)

**Select the $x$-values at which $h$ has an infinite discontinuity.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: An infinite discontinuity occurs at $x$-values where neither one-sided limit exists because each is unbounded.

![Function h is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 3 curves and a closed circle. The first curve starts at about (negative 8, negative 1) and moves downward toward a vertical asymptote at x = negative 6. The second curve moves upward away from the vertical asymptote at x = negative 6 to about (negative 4, negative 1) and then moves downward toward a vertical asymptote at x = negative 2. The third curve moves downward away from the vertical asymptote at x = negative 2, through about (1, 0), moves upward through about (2, 0) and an open circle at (4, 4), moves downward through about (7, negative 4), and ends in quadrant 4. The closed circle is plotted at (4, negative 2).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/95714a632ccc4db535abc785a374c85462a0a031)

Looking at the graph we can see that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -6^-}h(x)$ and $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -6^+}h(x)$ both approach negative infinity.

Therefore, $h$ has an infinite discontinuity at $x=-6$.

Similarly, we can see that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2^-}h(x)$ approaches negative infinity while $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2^+}h(x)$ approaches positive infinity.

Therefore, $h$ has an infinite discontinuity at $x=-2$.

$h$ has a removable discontinuity at $x=4$.

So this answer is incorrect.
In conclusion, $x=-6$ and $x=-2$ are the only values where $h$ has infinite discontinuities.

assessment_items.question_content:
This is the graph of function $f$. Dashed lines represent asymptotes.

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 3 curves. The first curve moves upward away from a horizontal asymptote at y = negative 1 to about (negative 3, 2), moves downward, and ends at an open circle at (1, negative 1). The second curve starts at an open circle at (1, 3) and moves downward toward a vertical asymptote at x = 5. The third curve moves downward away from the vertical asymptote at x = 5 and toward a horizontal asymptote at y = 3.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ccef16b56a6b47246f64ee0e266a17345be0166b)

**Select the $x$-values at which $f$ has an infinite discontinuity.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: An infinite discontinuity occurs at $x$-values where neither one-sided limit exists because each is unbounded.

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 3 curves. The first curve moves upward away from a horizontal asymptote at y = negative 1 to about (negative 3, 2), moves downward, and ends at an open circle at (1, negative 1). The second curve starts at an open circle at (1, 3) and moves downward toward a vertical asymptote at x = 5. The third curve moves downward away from the vertical asymptote at x = 5 and toward a horizontal asymptote at y = 3.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ccef16b56a6b47246f64ee0e266a17345be0166b)

Looking at the graph we can see that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 5^-}f(x)$ approaches negative infinity while $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 5^+}f(x)$ approaches positive infinity.

Therefore, $f$ has an infinite discontinuity at $x=5$.

$f$ is continuous at $x=-1$ and has a jump discontinuity at $x=1$.

So these answers are incorrect.
In conclusion, $x=5$ is the only value among the choices where $f$ has an infinite discontinuity.

The second curve moves upward away from the vertical asymptote at x = 2 to about (4, negative 2.8), moves downward through an open circle at (6, negative 3), and ends in quadrant 4.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3346424d818078b471b543b9fe5b5095f8ea5018)

**Select the $x$-values at which $g$ has an infinite discontinuity.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: An infinite discontinuity occurs at $x$-values where neither one-sided limit exists because each is unbounded.

![Function g is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 2 curves. The first curve starts in quadrant 2, moves downward through an open circle at about (negative 5, 3.2), to about (0, 1.8), and then moves upward toward a vertical asymptote at x = 2. The second curve moves upward away from the vertical asymptote at x = 2 to about (4, negative 2.8), moves downward through an open circle at (6, negative 3), and ends in quadrant 4.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3346424d818078b471b543b9fe5b5095f8ea5018)

Looking at the graph we can see that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2^-}g(x)$ approaches positive infinity while $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2^+}g(x)$ approaches negative infinity.

Therefore, $g$ has an infinite discontinuity at $x=2$.

$g$ has removable discontinuities at $x=-5$ and at $x=6$.

So these answers are incorrect.
In conclusion, $x=2$ is the only value where $g$ has an infinite discontinuity.

assessment_items.question_content:
This is the graph of function $h$. Dashed lines represent asymptotes.

![Function h is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 2 curves. The first curve moves upward away from a horizontal asymptote at y = 2 to (negative 3, 7), moves downward to an open circle at (1, negative 5), moves upward to about (4.8, negative 2.2), moves downward, and ends at an open circle at (6, negative 3). The second curve starts at an open circle at (6, negative 1) and moves upward toward the horizontal asymptote at y = 2.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2706aacc32eddce7184c65289de207a8e1182b8d)

**Select the $x$-values at which $h$ has an infinite discontinuity.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: An infinite discontinuity occurs at $x$-values where neither one-sided limit exists because each is unbounded.

![Function h is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 8 to 8. The graph consists of 2 curves. The first curve moves upward away from a horizontal asymptote at y = 2 to (negative 3, 7), moves downward to an open circle at (1, negative 5), moves upward to about (4.8, negative 2.2), moves downward, and ends at an open circle at (6, negative 3). The second curve starts at an open circle at (6, negative 1) and moves upward toward the horizontal asymptote at y = 2.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2706aacc32eddce7184c65289de207a8e1182b8d)

Looking at the graph we can see that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 1}h(x)=-5$ while $h(1)$ is undefined.

Therefore, $h$ has a removable discontinuity, not an infinite discontinuity, at $x=1$.

$h$ is continuous at $x=2$ and has a jump discontinuity at $x=6$.

So these answers are also incorrect.

In conclusion, none of the given $x$-values corresponds to where $h$ has an infinite discontinuity.

The answer is none of the above.
